---
title: "Foundations: variance, families, and penalties"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
This is rung 0 — the page that turns "I know linear algebra" into "I can read the
rest of this book as one story." Three ideas underpin the whole suite:

1. **We model variance, not just the mean.** Spread is the quantity of interest,
   and when data come in groups it *splits* into named buckets — the
   *variance components* that mixed models estimate.
2. **One exponential-family template** covers Gaussian, binary, count and positive
   responses, and it dictates exactly how each observation is weighted: the *mean
   determines the variance*.
3. **A smoothing penalty, a Gaussian prior, a random-effect variance, and a GP
   kernel are four names for one object.** That single equivalence is why six
   models share one engine instead of being six separate programs.

If you read only one page before the ladder, read this one.
:::

This suite is written for a reader who is comfortable with **linear algebra** —
matrices, least squares, eigendecomposition, rank, transposes — but who has had
**no formal training in statistics or numerical methods**. Everything here is
built up from the linear algebra you already own. Our aim across the book is to
carry you from *novice* (you can use a fitter and pick the right model) to
*practitioner* (you understand why each fit converges and what each parameter
means) to *developer* (you could implement the solver yourself). This page lays
the three planks that the whole climb rests on.

## The builder's through-line {#sec-throughline}

Here is the single sentence that the rest of the book elaborates. **Every model in
`nitrix.stats` is the same three-part object:**

1. a **linear predictor** $\eta = X\beta$ — a design matrix times a coefficient
   vector, the part you already know from ordinary least squares;
2. a **noise / penalty structure** — how the observations scatter around that
   predictor, and how strongly the coefficients are pulled toward zero or toward
   each other;
3. a **procedure to choose that structure's parameters** — the variances,
   dispersions and smoothing strengths that part (2) introduced.

Each rung of the ladder keeps part (1) untouched and *enriches part (2)*, then
leans on a shared part (3) to fit the new parameters. A GLM adds a non-Gaussian
noise shape; an LME adds correlated noise from grouping; a GAM adds a roughness
penalty; a GLMM combines two of those; a GAMM combines all of them; an HSGP writes
a kernel as one more penalty. The ladder is therefore not six topics but one
object, viewed at six levels of structural richness — and three planks support all
six. This page builds those three planks: **variance** (§3), the
**exponential family** (§4), and the **grand equivalence** (§5). With them in hand
the rest of the book reads as one argument.

A note on what "fitting" even means here. `nitrix.stats` is **mass-univariate**:
the same design $X$ is applied to *every* element of an image — every voxel,
vertex, or fixel — at once, hundreds of thousands of tiny regressions sharing one
design. That shapes the numerics (more in §6), but conceptually nothing changes:
each element is one instance of the three-part object.

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Hold one concrete question in mind for the whole page. Suppose we measure
**cortical thickness** at a patch of cortex in a group of people, several
measurements per person (repeat scans, or neighbouring vertices), and we want to
know: *does thickness change with age?*

A naive analysis regresses thickness on age and reads off a slope. But three things
about this data are not captured by "fit a line":

- The measurements **cluster by subject** — two scans of the same person are more
  alike than two scans of different people. The scatter is not one homogeneous
  cloud; it has structure we must name and model. (This is §3, and the seed of
  mixed models.)
- If instead of thickness we measured a **count** (number of lesions) or a
  **probability** (is this voxel "activated", yes/no), the spread would no longer
  be constant — it would *depend on the mean*. We need a noise model that adapts.
  (This is §4, and the engine of GLMs.)
- The age effect might not be a straight line but a **smooth curve**, and we must
  decide *how wiggly* to let it be without overfitting. The knob that controls
  wiggliness turns out to be the same knob as "how much do subjects vary." (This
  is §5, and the spine of the whole suite.)

Each plank answers one of these, and the planks turn out to be deeply connected.

## Variance: the quantity we actually model {#sec-variance}

### The mean is only half the story

You already have a working picture of **variance**: it is the average squared
distance of the data from their mean — a single number that says *how spread out*
the values are. For a sample $y_1,\dots,y_N$ with mean $\bar y$,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(y) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i-\bar y)^2 .
$$ {#eq-variance}

Regression, as you first met it, is about the **mean**: the fitted line predicts
$\bar y$ as a function of the covariates. But the line is only half the model. The
*other* half is how the data scatter about it — and that scatter is where almost
all of statistics lives. The mean tells you *where*; the variance tells you *how
confident you may be*, *how surprising* a new point is, and — crucially for us —
*what structure the noise has*. The whole rest of this book is, in one phrase,
**bookkeeping of variance**.

### When data come in groups, variance splits

Here is the move that turns a bag of numbers into a model. Suppose the
measurements come in **groups** — in our example, the repeated thickness
measurements *grouped by subject*. Look at @fig-foundations-varcomp. Two kinds of
spread are visible at once:

- **within-group spread**: how much one subject's repeated measurements scatter
  about *that subject's own mean* (the blue spans);
- **between-group spread**: how much the *subject means* scatter about the *grand
  mean* (the red span).

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-varcomp
#| fig-cap: "Cortical thickness for five subjects, several measurements each. The blue spans are within-subject spread (scatter about each subject's own mean, red dashes); the red span on the left is between-subject spread (scatter of the subject means about the grand mean, grey dashed line). Total variability is the sum of the two."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Cortical thickness (mm) for 5 subjects, several measurements each.
n_subj = 5
n_per = 8
subj_means = 2.6 + 0.35 * rng.standard_normal(n_subj)  # between-subject spread
within_sd = 0.12                                        # within-subject spread

vals = []
groups = []
for s in range(n_subj):
    v = subj_means[s] + within_sd * rng.standard_normal(n_per)
    vals.append(v)
    groups.append(np.full(n_per, s))
vals = np.concatenate(vals)
groups = np.concatenate(groups)

grand_mean = vals.mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.0))
ax.axhline(grand_mean, color="0.5", ls="--", lw=1.2, label="grand mean")

for s in range(n_subj):
    x = s + 1
    yv = vals[groups == s]
    gm = yv.mean()
    ax.scatter(np.full_like(yv, x) + 0.04 * rng.standard_normal(yv.size), yv,
               color="C0", s=22, zorder=3)
    ax.scatter([x], [gm], color="C3", s=90, marker="_", zorder=4, lw=2.5)
    ax.vlines(x + 0.28, yv.min(), yv.max(), color="C0", lw=2, alpha=0.6)

gmeans = np.array([vals[groups == s].mean() for s in range(n_subj)])
ax.vlines(0.4, gmeans.min(), gmeans.max(), color="C3", lw=3)
ax.text(0.5, gmeans.mean(), "between\n(SSB)", color="C3", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.text(n_subj + 0.55, grand_mean, "within\n(SSW)", color="C0", va="center", fontsize=9)

ax.set_xticks(range(1, n_subj + 1))
ax.set_xticklabels([f"subj {s+1}" for s in range(n_subj)])
ax.set_ylabel("cortical thickness (mm)")
ax.set_title("Total variance splits into between- and within-group parts")
ax.set_xlim(0.0, n_subj + 1.3)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Now write the total spread as a sum of squared distances from the grand mean. With
$y_{ij}$ the $j$-th measurement in group $i$, $\bar y_i$ the group mean, and
$\bar y$ the grand mean, add and subtract the group mean inside each term —
$y_{ij}-\bar y = (y_{ij}-\bar y_i) + (\bar y_i - \bar y)$ — and square. The cross
term sums to zero (within each group, the deviations about $\bar y_i$ cancel by
definition), leaving the clean identity

$$
\underbrace{\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij}-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SST, total}}
\;=\;
\underbrace{\sum_{i,j}(\bar y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SSB, between}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\sum_{i,j}(y_{ij}-\bar y_i)^2}_{\text{SSW, within}} .
$$ {#eq-anova}

This is the **ANOVA identity**, and it is the most important picture on this page.
Total variability is *exactly* between-group variability plus within-group
variability — no remainder. Read it as a statement about *attributing* spread: some
of the variation is because subjects differ from one another (SSB), and the rest is
because a single subject's measurements differ among themselves (SSW).

### A variance component is a labelled bucket of variance

The payoff is a definition you will use for the rest of the book. A **variance
component** is *a labelled bucket of variance tied to a grouping factor.* In
@fig-foundations-varcomp there are two buckets: a "between-subjects" component and a
"within-subject (residual)" component. Estimating their sizes — "how much of the
spread is subject-to-subject vs. measurement-to-measurement" — is precisely what a
**linear mixed-effects model** does. We will write the between-subjects bucket as a
random-effect variance and the within-subject bucket as the residual variance; the
LME chapter formalises this and the variance-component parameters live in $\theta$
([the canonical notation](#sec-notation)). The ANOVA identity is the seed: a mixed
model is the ANOVA identity made continuous, with a covariate (age) carried along.

### The simplest decomposition you already know: $R^2$

You have actually met variance decomposition before, under a different name. When
you fit a line by least squares, the total spread of $y$ splits into a part the fit
**explains** and a part it leaves as **residual**:

$$
\underbrace{\sum_i (y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{total}}
=
\underbrace{\sum_i (\hat y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{explained}}
+
\underbrace{\sum_i (y_i-\hat y_i)^2}_{\text{residual}},
\qquad
R^2 = \frac{\text{explained}}{\text{total}} .
$$ {#eq-rsq}

This is structurally the *same* identity as @eq-anova — "explained" plays the role
of "between" and "residual" the role of "within" — and $R^2$ is the fraction of
variance the model accounts for. Whenever a later chapter says it "decomposes
variance" or "estimates a variance component," it is doing a richer version of this
one move you already trust.

## Exponential families: one template, many noise models {#sec-expfam}

### Why one template

Our second motivating wrinkle was that the *noise shape* depends on what we
measured. Gaussian scatter (cortical thickness) is symmetric and its spread does
not depend on the mean. Counts (lesions) are non-negative integers whose spread
*grows* with the mean. A yes/no label (activated?) is a coin flip whose variance is
largest at probability $\tfrac12$ and zero at the extremes. We do not want a
separate theory for each. We want one **template** that has all of these as special
cases, so a single fitting routine can serve them all. That template is the
**exponential family**, and it is exactly the object `_family.py` implements.

### The actual `Family` object

In `nitrix.stats`, a noise model is a `Family` — a frozen, hashable record of pure
functions (so it can ride as a static argument through `vmap`/JIT). Reading
`src/nitrix/stats/_family.py`, the fields that matter to us are:

- `link` / `linkinv` — the link $g(\mu)$ and its inverse $g^{-1}(\eta)$, mapping
  between the linear predictor $\eta$ and the mean $\mu$;
- `variance` — the **variance function** $V(\mu)$, the spread *as a function of the
  mean*;
- `unit_deviance` — the per-observation **deviance** contribution $d(y,\mu)$;
- `loglik` — the per-observation log-likelihood;
- `has_fixed_dispersion` — whether the dispersion $\phi$ is fixed at $1$ (Binomial,
  Poisson) or estimated (Gaussian, Gamma).

The built-in registry holds `GAUSSIAN`, `BINOMIAL`, `POISSON`, `GAMMA`,
`NEGBINOMIAL` and `TWEEDIE`; `negbinomial(alpha)` and `tweedie(p)` are factories,
and `Family.with_link(...)` swaps in a non-canonical link (`'probit'`, `'cloglog'`,
`'sqrt'`, `'inverse'`, …) while leaving the distribution untouched. Everything
below explains *why* those particular fields are the right ones.

### Building the canonical form from intuition

Start from a wish: *one density formula whose shape is steered by a single
parameter.* Write a generic density and ask what the simplest such "steerable"
family looks like. If we want the steering parameter $\theta$ — call it the
**natural parameter** — to enter in the cleanest possible way, we put it *linearly*
in the exponent, multiplying the data $y$:

$$
f(y;\theta,\phi)=\exp\!\left\{\frac{y\,\theta - b(\theta)}{\phi} + c(y,\phi)\right\} .
$$ {#eq-expfam}

Let us name every piece, because each one earns its keep:

- $\theta$ is the **natural parameter** — the one dial that moves the distribution.
- $y\theta$ is the *only* place the data and the dial interact, and they interact
  linearly. This single design choice is what makes the whole GLM machinery work.
- $b(\theta)$ is the **cumulant function** (or log-partition). It is not free: it is
  whatever makes the density integrate to $1$. As we are about to see, this one
  normalising job forces $b$ to encode *both* the mean and the variance.
- $\phi$ is the **dispersion** — an overall scale on the noise (variance for the
  Gaussian; fixed at $1$ for Poisson/Binomial).
- $c(y,\phi)$ collects the terms that depend only on $y$ and $\phi$, not on
  $\theta$. It makes the density normalise but carries no information about the
  mean, so it never touches the fitting of $\beta$.

That is the entire template. Its power is that the unknown of interest enters as
the bare product $y\theta$; everything model-specific is bundled into the one
function $b$.

### The single fact: the mean determines the variance

Now the derivation that makes @eq-expfam pay off — and it is short, so we show every
step. A density integrates to one for *every* value of $\theta$:

$$
\int f(y;\theta,\phi)\,dy = 1 \quad\text{for all }\theta .
$$

Because this holds for all $\theta$, we may differentiate both sides with respect
to $\theta$ and the right-hand side stays $0$. Differentiating once under the
integral, and using $\partial_\theta f = \frac{y-b'(\theta)}{\phi}\,f$ (the
chain rule applied to the exponent in @eq-expfam), gives
$\int \frac{y-b'(\theta)}{\phi} f\,dy = 0$, i.e. $\mathbb{E}[y] = b'(\theta)$.
Differentiating a *second* time and simplifying gives the variance. The two results
are:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}[y] = \mu = b'(\theta), \qquad
\operatorname{Var}[y] = \phi\,b''(\theta) = \phi\,V(\mu).\;}
$$ {#eq-meanvar}

Read what just happened. The mean is the *first* derivative of $b$, and the variance
is the *second* derivative of the same $b$ — so once you fix the mean, the variance
is no longer free. Define the **variance function** $V(\mu) = b''(\theta(\mu))$, the
variance written as a function of the mean. Then the entire noise behaviour is
captured by $V(\mu)$ and the scalar $\phi$. This is *the* foundational fact of the
GLM world:

> In an exponential family, **the mean determines the variance.**

It is exactly why count data far from zero is noisier than count data near zero (for
Poisson, $V(\mu)=\mu$), and why a fair-coin probability is the noisiest (for
Bernoulli, $V(\mu)=\mu(1-\mu)$ peaks at $\mu=\tfrac12$). And it is the fact that
fixes the **IRLS weights** in the GLM chapter: an observation is weighted by how
informative it is, which is inversely related to its variance. Concretely, the IRLS
core in `_irls.py` forms the working weight `w = mu_eta**2 / V(mu)` and working
response `z = eta + (y - mu) / mu_eta` — see
[the working-response derivation](glm.qmd#sec-working). The weight is small exactly
where $V(\mu)$ is large; @eq-meanvar is what tells you where that is.

### The mean–variance curve, seen

@fig-foundations-meanvar draws $V(\mu)$ for the four classic families on one axis.
The picture *is* the intuition: the Gaussian curve is flat (constant spread), the
Poisson rises linearly (noisier where the count is larger), the Bernoulli arches and
returns to zero at the certain outcomes, and the Gamma rises quadratically
(multiplicative noise — a 10% error, not a fixed-size error).

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-meanvar
#| fig-cap: "The variance function V(mu) for four exponential families. Gaussian is flat (spread independent of the mean); Poisson grows like mu (counts far from zero are noisier); Bernoulli is mu(1-mu), largest at a fair coin and zero at a sure outcome; Gamma is mu^2 (constant *relative* noise). The mean determines the variance — these curves are that fact, drawn."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mu = np.linspace(0.01, 3.0, 400)
mu01 = np.linspace(0.01, 0.99, 400)  # Bernoulli mean lives in (0, 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.2))
ax.plot(mu, np.ones_like(mu), label="Gaussian:  V = 1 (constant)", lw=2)
ax.plot(mu, mu, label=r"Poisson:  V = $\mu$", lw=2)
ax.plot(mu01, mu01 * (1.0 - mu01), label=r"Bernoulli:  V = $\mu(1-\mu)$", lw=2)
ax.plot(mu, mu ** 2, label=r"Gamma:  V = $\mu^2$", lw=2)

ax.set_xlabel(r"mean response  $\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"variance function  $V(\mu)$")
ax.set_title("The mean dictates the variance (one curve per family)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 3.2)
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The canonical link

The link $g$ connects the linear predictor to the mean: $\eta = g(\mu)$, equivalently
$\mu = g^{-1}(\eta)$. We always have $\eta = X\beta$, but $\mu$ may live on a
restricted range — a probability in $(0,1)$, a positive rate in $(0,\infty)$ — and the
link is what bends the unbounded line onto that range. Among all valid links, one is
special: the **canonical link** is the $g$ that makes the natural parameter equal the
linear predictor, $\theta=\eta$. Why care? Because @eq-expfam couples $y$ to the model
*only* through $y\theta$; choosing $\theta=\eta=X\beta$ makes the algebra of fitting
maximally clean (the Fisher-scoring weights simplify, $X^{\top} y$ becomes a sufficient
statistic). For the Gaussian the canonical link is the identity ($\mu=\eta$); for
Poisson it is the log ($\mu=e^{\eta}$, keeping rates positive); for Bernoulli it is the
logit ($\mu=\operatorname{expit}(\eta)$, keeping probabilities in $(0,1)$). These are
exactly the defaults baked into `GAUSSIAN`, `POISSON`, `BINOMIAL`. A non-canonical link
(probit, cloglog) is perfectly legal — `Family.with_link` swaps it in — it gives up
only that one bit of algebraic convenience.

### The deviance: a family's natural badness-of-fit

How well does a fitted $\mu$ match the data $y$? For Gaussian noise the natural answer
is the sum of squared residuals — but that is *specific* to the Gaussian. The exponential
family gives a measure that adapts to each noise shape. Imagine the best possible fit:
one that sets $\hat\mu_i = y_i$ for every observation (the **saturated** model — it bends
to every point). It has the highest log-likelihood any model could achieve on this data.
The **deviance** is twice the gap between that ceiling and your model's log-likelihood:

$$
D = 2\big(\ell_{\text{saturated}} - \ell_{\text{model}}\big)
  = \sum_i d(y_i,\mu_i),
$$ {#eq-deviance}

a sum of non-negative per-observation **unit deviances** $d(y,\mu)$ — exactly the
`unit_deviance` field of `Family`. The deviance is the family's own notion of
"sum of squared residuals": for the Gaussian, $d(y,\mu)=(y-\mu)^2$ and the deviance *is*
the residual sum of squares (look at `GAUSSIAN.unit_deviance` — it is literally
`(y - mu) ** 2`). For the Poisson it is the count-aware
$2\big(y\log(y/\mu)-(y-\mu)\big)$, and for the Binomial the log-odds form
$2\big(y\log\tfrac{y}{\mu}+(1-y)\log\tfrac{1-y}{1-\mu}\big)$ — both readable straight off
`_poisson_deviance` and `_binomial_deviance`. Deviance is what IRLS drives downhill, and
what model-comparison criteria (AIC/BIC) are built from.

### The four families at a glance

Putting @eq-meanvar to work, each family is pinned down by its cumulant $b(\theta)$, which
fixes $\mu=b'(\theta)$, the variance function $V(\mu)=b''$, and the canonical link:

| Family | $b(\theta)$ | $\mu=b'(\theta)$ | $V(\mu)$ | Canonical link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gaussian | $\theta^2/2$ | $\theta$ | $1$ | identity |
| Bernoulli | $\log(1+e^{\theta})$ | $\dfrac{e^{\theta}}{1+e^{\theta}}$ | $\mu(1-\mu)$ | logit |
| Poisson | $e^{\theta}$ | $e^{\theta}$ | $\mu$ | log |
| Gamma | $-\log(-\theta)$ | $-1/\theta$ | $\mu^2$ | inverse (`nitrix` defaults to log) |

(Read each row left to right as a worked instance of @eq-meanvar: differentiate $b$ once
to get $\mu$, twice to get $V$.) `nitrix`'s `GAMMA` uses a **log** link by default — the
practical choice for positive measures like reaction times or volumes — rather than the
canonical inverse link; the canonical link is available by building a custom `Family`.
The same `_family.py` also ships overdispersed counts (`NEGBINOMIAL`, $V=\mu+\alpha\mu^2$)
and the semicontinuous `TWEEDIE` ($V=\mu^p$), each a point on the same template.

## The grand equivalence: penalty = prior = variance component = kernel {#sec-equivalence}

This is the conceptual payoff of the whole book, and it is worth slowing down for.
Our third motivating wrinkle was the wiggliness knob: how smooth should the
age-trajectory be? We will show that this knob is *the same object* as the
between-subjects variance from §3 — and as a Bayesian prior, and as a Gaussian-process
kernel. Four vocabularies, one matrix.

### Start with a penalty you would invent yourself

Suppose you fit a flexible curve as a linear combination of many basis functions,
$f(x)=\sum_k \beta_k\,\phi_k(x)$, so the curve is again $X\beta$ for a basis matrix
$X$. With many basis functions, plain least squares overfits — it chases every noisy
wiggle. The natural fix, which you could invent unaided, is to *add a cost for
wiggliness*: penalise the fit when the coefficients describe a rough curve. Measure
roughness with a quadratic form $\beta^{\top} S\beta$ (for a curvature penalty, $S$
is a second-difference operator — large when neighbouring coefficients jump around),
weight it by a knob $\lambda$, and minimise

$$
\lVert y - X\beta\rVert^2 \;+\; \lambda\,\beta^{\top} S\beta .
$$ {#eq-penalised}

Here $S$ is the **penalty matrix** (we always write the penalty matrix as $S$ in this book, never $P$),
and $\lambda$ is the **smoothing parameter**. Large $\lambda$ ⇒ wiggliness is
expensive ⇒ a flat fit; small $\lambda$ ⇒ wiggliness is cheap ⇒ a wiggly fit. So far
this is pure linear algebra: @eq-penalised is **ridge regression** with a shaped
penalty, and its solution is the penalised normal equations
$(X^{\top}X + \lambda S)\beta = X^{\top}y$ — the same solve `_irls.py` performs (with
weights) as $(X^{\top}WX + S)\beta = X^{\top}Wz$.

### The same objective is a negative log-posterior

Now read @eq-penalised through a *probabilistic* lens. Suppose the data are Gaussian
about the fit with variance $\sigma^2$, and — this is the new idea — suppose we hold a
prior belief that the coefficients are *small and smooth*, encoded as a Gaussian prior
$\beta \sim N(0,\,\tau^2 S^{-1})$. ($S^{-1}$ as a covariance means $S$ is the prior
*precision*: directions where $S$ is large are directions the prior keeps tight.) The
negative log-posterior is the negative log-likelihood plus the negative log-prior:

$$
-\log p(\beta\mid y)
\;=\;
\underbrace{\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\lVert y-X\beta\rVert^2}_{\text{from the Gaussian likelihood}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\frac{1}{2\tau^2}\,\beta^{\top} S\beta}_{\text{from the Gaussian prior}}
\;+\;\text{const} .
$$ {#eq-logpost}

Multiply through by $2\sigma^2$: this is *term for term* @eq-penalised, with

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{\tau^2} .
$$ {#eq-lambda-tau}

Stop and feel the weight of this. The penalised least-squares fit you would have
invented from scratch is **identically** the *maximum-a-posteriori* estimate of a
Bayesian model with a Gaussian prior. The penalty matrix $S$ **is** the prior
precision. The smoothing strength $\lambda$ **is** a *variance ratio*: noise variance
over prior variance. A large $\lambda$ (strong smoothing) is the same statement as a
small $\tau$ (a tight prior that insists the coefficients be small).

### …and a tight prior is a small random-effect variance

One more rename closes the loop back to §3. "Treat the coefficients as random,
drawn from $N(0,\tau^2 S^{-1})$" is the *definition* of a **random effect** with
variance component $\tau^2$. So:

- "shrink the wiggly coefficients" (penalty view, GAM), **is**
- "give them a tight prior" (Bayesian view, HSGP), **is**
- "treat them as a random effect with small variance" (mixed-model view, LME).

The smoothing parameter $\lambda$, the prior standard deviation $\tau$, and the
random-effect variance component $\theta$ are *the same number wearing three hats*.
@fig-foundations-equivalence makes this literal: a single sequence of fits, drawn
once and labelled twice — once by the penalty strength $\lambda$, once by the
equivalent prior standard deviation $\tau=\sigma/\sqrt{\lambda}$. Same curves; two
vocabularies.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-equivalence
#| fig-cap: "One smoothing fit, two labels. The same three curves (a smooth recovered from noisy data via a roughness-penalised basis fit) are shown twice. Left: labelled by the penalty strength lambda — a roughness cost. Right: labelled by the equivalent prior standard deviation tau = sigma / sqrt(lambda) — a Bayesian prior width. Strong penalty (large lambda, red) is the same fit as a tight prior (small tau): penalty strength and prior width are one knob, related by lambda = sigma^2 / tau^2."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A noisy nonlinear signal: a stand-in for a smooth age-trajectory to recover.
n = 60
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
f_true = np.sin(2 * np.pi * x) + 0.4 * np.sin(6 * np.pi * x)
sigma = 0.45
y = f_true + sigma * rng.standard_normal(n)

# A dense Gaussian-bump basis with a second-difference roughness penalty S.
K = 24
centres = np.linspace(-0.05, 1.05, K)
width = 0.06
B = np.exp(-0.5 * ((x[:, None] - centres[None, :]) / width) ** 2)

D = np.zeros((K - 2, K))
for i in range(K - 2):
    D[i, i] = 1.0
    D[i, i + 1] = -2.0
    D[i, i + 2] = 1.0
S = D.T @ D  # the penalty matrix

BtB = B.T @ B
Bty = B.T @ y


def fit(lam):
    coef = np.linalg.solve(BtB + lam * S, Bty)
    return B @ coef


lams = [0.05, 2.0, 80.0]
colors = ["C0", "C2", "C3"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0), sharey=True)

ax = axes[0]
ax.scatter(x, y, s=14, color="0.6", zorder=1, label="data")
for lam, c in zip(lams, colors):
    ax.plot(x, fit(lam), color=c, lw=2.2, label=fr"$\lambda={lam:g}$")
ax.set_title(r"Penalty view: tune $\lambda$ (roughness cost)")
ax.set_xlabel("age (scaled)")
ax.set_ylabel("response")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right")

ax = axes[1]
ax.scatter(x, y, s=14, color="0.6", zorder=1)
for lam, c in zip(lams, colors):
    tau = sigma / np.sqrt(lam)
    ax.plot(x, fit(lam), color=c, lw=2.2, label=fr"$\tau={tau:.2f}$")
ax.set_title(r"Prior view: tune $\tau$ (prior sd of coefficients)")
ax.set_xlabel("age (scaled)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right")

fig.suptitle(r"One fit, two labels:  $\lambda=\sigma^2/\tau^2$", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.95))
plt.show()
```

### From coefficients to functions: the kernel

There is a fourth hat, and it is what turns this from a curiosity into the HSGP
chapter. A prior on the *coefficients* $\beta$ induces, through $f=X\beta$, a prior
on the *function values* $f$. Push the coefficient covariance $\tau^2 S^{-1}$ through
the basis: the function values have covariance
$\operatorname{Cov}(f) = X\,(\tau^2 S^{-1})\,X^{\top}$. A covariance *between the
values of a function at different inputs* is precisely a **kernel**, and a function
with a Gaussian prior over its values is a **Gaussian process**. So the penalty $S$,
read as a prior on coefficients, *is* a GP kernel in disguise — and a clever choice of
basis ($X$) and penalty ($S$) reproduces a desired kernel cheaply. That is the
Hilbert-space Gaussian process; see [the HSGP chapter](hsgp.qmd).

### Why this is the spine of the suite

State it plainly, because it is the reason the library exists in one piece rather than
six:

> A smoothing **penalty** (GAM), a Gaussian **prior** (Bayes/HSGP), a random-effect
> **variance component** (LME), and a GP **kernel** are four names for one object —
> a quadratic form $\beta^{\top} S\beta$ with a scale parameter.

Because the object is shared, the *machinery* is shared. GAM, LME, GLMM, GAMM and
HSGP are one engine, not five programs: each fits a penalised weighted normal-equations
system and chooses the scale parameter by a (restricted) likelihood. The next section
names that shared machinery.

## The shared computational substrate {#sec-substrate}

Everything above reduces, computationally, to two moves repeated across the suite:

1. **Solve a (penalised) weighted normal-equations system.** Every model ends at
   $(X^{\top} W X + S)\,\beta = X^{\top} W z$ — a small $p\times p$ linear solve you
   recognise from least squares, with weights $W$ from the family (§4) and a penalty
   $S$ from the equivalence (§5). The numerics of doing this stably — Cholesky,
   damped Newton, the cuSOLVER-free dense inverse `small_inv_logdet`, block-Woodbury
   for many groups — live in [the solvers page](solvers.qmd).
2. **Choose the variance/smoothing parameters** $\theta,\lambda$ by maximising a
   **(restricted) likelihood** with a cheap iteration. The multiplicative
   Fellner–Schall update that does this is shared across GAM/LME/GLMM/GAMM; see
   [Choosing smoothing parameters](fellner-schall.qmd).

Two design constraints from the mass-univariate setting colour both moves. Because one
shared design $X$ is fit to an entire image at once, the expensive shared pieces — a
factorisation of $X$, an eigendecomposition of the random-effect design — are computed
**once** and reused across all elements; only the per-element response changes. And
because each fit must `vmap`/JIT across hundreds of thousands of elements, every fit runs
a **fixed number of iterations** (no data-dependent stopping) and every $p\times p$ solve
avoids the vendor GPU solver (`cuSOLVER`, which is unreliable on some accelerators) — that
is why `_irls.py` routes every solve through the in-house `small_inv_logdet`.

## Notation {#sec-notation}

The whole suite uses these symbols. Other chapters point back here; keep this table
nearby on a first read.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $y$, $Y$ | response vector; mass-univariate response matrix ($V\times N$: $V$ elements, $N$ observations) |
| $N$, $p$ | number of observations; number of fixed-effect coefficients |
| $X$, $\beta$ | fixed-effects design ($N\times p$); fixed-effect coefficients |
| $Z$, $b$ | random-effects design ($N\times q$); random-effect coefficients |
| $\eta=X\beta$ | linear predictor |
| $g$, $\mu=g^{-1}(\eta)$ | link function; mean response |
| $W$, $z$ | IRLS working weights (diagonal); IRLS working response |
| $V(\mu)$, $\phi$ | variance function; dispersion parameter |
| $S$, $\lambda$ | **penalty matrix** (roughness/precision); smoothing parameter(s) — always $S$, never $P$ |
| $\theta$ | variance components / covariance parameters (the things (RE)ML estimates) |
| $G$, $R$ | random-effect covariance ($q\times q$); residual covariance ($N\times N$) |
| $V_y = ZGZ^{\top}+R$ | marginal (observation) covariance |
| $\top$, $\operatorname{tr}$ | transpose; trace |

## How to read this suite {#sec-how-to-read}

Three paths, matched to the novice → practitioner → developer arc:

- **Novice (fast path).** Read this page, then each chapter's TL;DR callout, plus the
  GLM chapter in full. That is enough to choose the right model for a question and to
  use the fitters.
- **Practitioner (ladder order).** Read in ladder order — foundations → glm → lme →
  gam → glmm → gamm → hsgp — detouring to [solvers](solvers.qmd) and
  [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) when an algorithm recurs. You will understand
  why each fit converges and what every parameter means.
- **Developer (source-linked deep read).** Read the ladder *and* follow each
  **See also** link into `src/nitrix/stats/`. The pages are written so that the prose
  maps onto the actual functions (`Family`, `fit_penalised_irls`, `small_inv_logdet`,
  the Fellner–Schall update), so you could reimplement a rung from the chapter.

## Further reading

- McCullagh, P. & Nelder, J. A. (1989). *Generalized Linear Models* (2nd ed.).
  Chapman & Hall/CRC. The canonical reference for exponential families, links, and
  deviance.
- Efron, B. & Hastie, T. (2016). *Computer Age Statistical Inference*. Cambridge
  University Press. Clear modern treatment of exponential families and deviance
  (and much else); freely available from the authors.
- Wood, S. N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R*
  (2nd ed.). Chapman & Hall/CRC. The definitive source for the penalty ⇔ random-effect
  ⇔ prior view, and the engine that unifies GAM/LME/GAMM.
- Pinheiro, J. C. & Bates, D. M. (2000). *Mixed-Effects Models in S and S-PLUS*.
  Springer. Variance components and the structure of grouped data.
- Gelman, A. & Hill, J. (2007). *Data Analysis Using Regression and
  Multilevel/Hierarchical Models*. Cambridge University Press. An applied,
  intuition-first account of variance decomposition and multilevel structure.
- Rasmussen, C. E. & Williams, C. K. I. (2006). *Gaussian Processes for Machine
  Learning*. MIT Press. Kernels as priors over functions; the GP end of the
  equivalence. Freely available at gaussianprocess.org/gpml.

## See also

- Source: [`_family.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_family.py)
  — the `Family` record, the built-in families, links, variance functions and
  deviances described in §4.
- Source: [`_irls.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_irls.py)
  — the penalised IRLS core that performs the shared $(X^{\top}WX+S)\beta=X^{\top}Wz$
  solve.
- Next rung: [Generalised linear models — the foundation](glm.qmd) *(start the ladder
  here)*.
- Shared machinery: [Solvers](solvers.qmd) and
  [Choosing smoothing parameters: the Fellner–Schall method](fellner-schall.qmd).
